local_names.japanese: 牛しゃぶカレーライス

aliases: 牛しゃぶカレーライス
Japanese Beef Curry Rice with Shabu Beef

english: Japanese Beef Curry Rice with Shabu Beef
japanese: 牛しゃぶカレーライス

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general curry rice class (FOODON_00002809); no specific class for Japanese beef shabu curry rice exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (curry with meat and rice) is the closest defensible match; no specific code for shabu-style beef curry rice exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for beef shabu curry rice. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual dish preparations at this level of specificity.

allergens: soy (may be present in curry roux)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a mound of steamed white rice topped with Japanese curry sauce and shabu-blanched beef slices, as typically served in a Japanese curry restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Shabu Curry Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef Shabu Curry Rice (cr_id G00010937, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef and curry components — see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database — white rice and curry analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beef Shabu Curry Rice. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component being steamed white rice (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the curry sauce, dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g), protein (6.5 g/100g), and fat (4.21 g/100g), which collectively lower the composite dish GI. Analogous Japanese curry rice dishes are reported in the medium GI range (60–70) in peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (65 × 100.76) / 100 = 65.5 per 400 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g (borderline), partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g and dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt curry preparation and avoid adding soy sauce or other high-sodium condiments, as the 231 mg/100g sodium content can accumulate significantly over a 400 g serving (approximately 924 mg sodium per plate, approaching 40% of the 2,300 mg daily limit); the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio and high dietary fibre provide partial DASH-diet offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~65) from white rice base, moderated by 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre and 6.5 g/100g protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to reduce glycaemic load, as the white rice base carries a medium GI (~65) and a 400 g serving delivers approximately 101 g of available carbohydrate; the 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre and 6.5 g/100g protein from shabu beef help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and curry roux; sodium 231 mg/100g; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100g in HPB source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be aware that the beef and curry roux contribute saturated fat (estimated ~1.8 g/100g) and the 231 mg/100g sodium adds cardiovascular load; requesting leaner beef cuts, reduced curry roux, and smaller portions (200 g) is advisable to keep saturated fat and sodium within heart-healthy limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef (red meat) is high in arachidonic acid — a pro-inflammatory omega-6 fatty acid; potato is a nightshade vegetable
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish because beef is rich in arachidonic acid which amplifies inflammatory pathways, and potato (a nightshade vegetable) may trigger individual RA flares; substituting beef with chicken, fish, or tofu and requesting reduced coconut milk in the curry sauce would substantially reduce the pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Shabu Curry Rice (牛しゃぶカレーライス, Gyū Shabu Karē Raisu) is a Japanese-style dish served in Singapore that combines two culinary traditions: the gentle shabu-shabu blanching technique for beef and the rich, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce served over steamed white rice. Thinly sliced beef is briefly blanched in hot water or light broth (shabu-shabu method), preserving its tenderness and keeping added fat low, before being placed atop a bed of steamed Japanese-style white rice and ladled with a thick Japanese curry sauce made from curry roux, potato, carrot, and onion. The dish delivers a comforting, mildly spiced flavour profile with a soft, starchy texture from the rice and potato, tender beef slices, and a savoury-sweet curry sauce. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium, with notable potassium (327 mg), calcium (54 mg), magnesium (40 mg), and iron (2.55 mg). The high dietary fibre content for a rice dish and the lean shabu preparation method make it a relatively moderate-glycaemic option compared to standard curry rice, though the white rice base still carries a medium GI (~65). Clinically, sodium content warrants attention for hypertension management, and the medium GI and carbohydrate load require portion control for type 2 diabetes. The beef component disqualifies this dish for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to arachidonic acid content. This dish is commonly found in Japanese curry restaurants and food courts across Singapore and is suitable for lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010937, confidence 0.95, provenance canonical, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy beef and curry components as HPB does not report this field for this entry. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database white rice and Japanese curry rice analogues. All other values are exact HPB matches.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for thinly sliced beef and Japanese curry sauce components, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this specific entry. This is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for G00010937, and no sufficiently close equivalent entry was found in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 mg/100g by HPB for this entry. This is unusual for a beef-containing dish and may reflect a reporting artefact or rounding in the HPB source; confirmed zero per HPB source. 4. Glycemic load per serving calculated using standard GL formula: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (65 × 100.76) / 100 = 65.5 per 400 g serving. Field glycemic_load_per_serving set to 65.5. 5. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002809 and FoodEx2 code A0CKW are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for Japanese beef shabu curry rice in either system as of 2026-04-16. 6. Halal status not confirmed; standard Japanese curry roux may contain pork-derived ingredients — consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the specific restaurant.
